metadata.notes: Content overhaul to Wingspan standard — Rogue B, March 2026. Research-corrected voyage structure, loot tokens, reputation overflow, score dial permanence. Sources: official Stonemaier rulebook, Dized official rules, BGG FAQ, r/boardgames strategy threads.

key_rulings.text: At the start of each voyage, deal 6 character cards to one player. All other players find and take the same 6 characters from their own decks.
Daytime abilities resolve from lowest rank to highest rank. Dusk abilities (loot collection) resolve from highest rank to lowest rank.
When two or more players play the same ranked character, the player with higher reputation resolves first.
Players keep unused character cards from previous voyages. Before each new voyage, 6 additional cards are dealt, so hands grow over the course of the game.
During dusk, you must take one loot token if any remain. You cannot voluntarily skip collecting loot.
Anchor abilities on characters in your ship resolve at the end of each voyage, after all days are complete.
Characters in every player's graveyard are visible to all players at all times.

At the end of each voyage, increase your score dial by your doubloon total, then discard those doubloons. The score dial total is permanent and cannot be lost. If your score exceeds 99 after the third voyage, set the dial to 99 and use doubloons to represent additional wealth.
Voyage 1 lasts 4 days, Voyage 2 lasts 5 days, and Voyage 3 lasts 6 days. The number of days increases each voyage regardless of player count.

If you gain more reputation than the track allows, gain 1 doubloon for each reputation you would have gained. If you lose more reputation than the track allows, lose 1 doubloon for each reputation you would have lost.
Each space on the reputation track displays a number representing doubloons earned by that player at the beginning of a new voyage, rewarding higher reputation positions.
Night abilities resolve after all loot has been collected during dusk. Night resolution order follows the same rank ordering as daytime.
All character abilities are mandatory. When a character's ability triggers at its designated time, you must resolve it — you cannot choose to skip it.

subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Board & Dials
- **1 double-sided island board** (508x406mm) — This is your shared play surface. One side is labeled **Calm**, the other **Storm**. Both sides show the same day track and loot spaces, but loot token effects change depending on the side. Use the Calm side for your first games (Storm adds nastier loot effects). It works for all player counts (1-6).
- **6 score dials** — One for each player. These circular dials track your doubloon total throughout the game. Rotate the dial window to update your score.
- **6 graveyard tiles** — One for each player. Your discarded characters go here face-up after each voyage. Everyone can inspect any graveyard at any time.

#### Cards
- **240 character cards** (40 unique characters x 6 identical sets) — Each player gets their own complete deck of 40 characters. Every character card shows a rank number (1-40), a name, and abilities marked with timing icons that tell you when they trigger during a day.
- **39 Automa cards** — These are for solo play only; set them aside in a multiplayer game.

#### Tokens
- **48 loot tokens** (bakelite) — These chunky tokens come in 7 types and get drawn randomly from the cloth bag each voyage. The types are: **Chest** (gold), **Map** (blue), **Amulet** (green), **Barrel** (brown), **Saber** (red), **Hook** (gray), and **Relic** (purple/cursed). What each token does depends on which board side you're playing.
- **7 loot tiles** — Quick-reference tiles showing what each loot token does. Keep these near the board.
- **6 reputation tokens** — One per player, placed on the reputation track on the island board.
- **54 doubloon tokens** — Physical coins you'll use alongside your score dials for tracking wealth.
- **4 Automa loot tiles** — Solo play only.

#### Other
- **1 midshipman token** — This little figure marks the first player.
- **1 cloth loot bag** — You'll draw loot tokens randomly from this each voyage.
- **1 rulebook + 1 solo rulebook**

subtopics.title: Game Structure & Setup
subtopics.content:
#### How the Game Works
Libertalia plays out over **3 voyages** that get longer as the game goes on: **Voyage 1 is 4 days, Voyage 2 is 5 days, and Voyage 3 is 6 days**. Each day, everyone simultaneously picks a character card from their hand and reveals it. Characters then resolve abilities and collect loot based on rank order. After all 3 voyages, whoever has the most **doubloons** wins.

Here's the twist that makes Libertalia special: **every player starts with the exact same characters**. The game becomes about outguessing your opponents — figuring out which character they'll play and when.

#### Setting Up
1. **Place the island board** in the center of the table. Use the **Calm side** for your first games (flip to **Storm** once you're comfortable for a more aggressive experience).
2. **Each player grabs:** 1 complete set of 40 character cards (sorted by number), 1 score dial (set it to 0), 1 graveyard tile, and 1 reputation token.
3. **Place all reputation tokens** on the reputation track — this track has 6 spaces. Stack everyone's tokens on the center space to start.
4. **Fill the loot bag** by dropping all 48 loot tokens into the cloth bag.
5. **Deal your starting characters:** Randomly draw 6 character cards from one player's deck. **Every other player finds those same 6 characters** in their own deck. You all begin with an identical 6-card hand.
6. **Prepare Voyage 1 loot:** Since Voyage 1 has 4 days, you need 4 rows of loot. For each day's row, draw loot tokens from the bag equal to the number of players and place them face-up in the matching row on the island board.
7. **Give the midshipman token** to a random player — they'll be first player.

#### Checkpoint
Each player should now have: the same 6 character cards in hand, a score dial set to 0, a graveyard tile, and a reputation token on the track. The island board (Calm side up for your first game) should display 4 rows of face-up loot tokens — one row per day, with tokens equal to the player count in each row.

subtopics.title: The Day Phases
subtopics.content:
#### Each Day Has 5 Phases
Every day within a voyage follows the same sequence. Here's what happens, step by step:

#### Phase 1: Sunrise — Pick Your Character
Everyone simultaneously chooses **1 character card** from their hand and places it **face-down** in front of them. Once all players are ready, flip your cards face-up at the same time.

If two or more players reveal the **same character** (same rank number), the player with **higher reputation** goes first among those tied characters. (This is one reason reputation matters even outside of scoring.)

#### Phase 2: Daytime — Day Abilities Resolve (Low to High)
Now check for **daytime abilities** (marked with a sun icon). These resolve from **lowest rank to highest** — meaning low-rank characters strike first. Daytime abilities tend to be aggressive: stealing doubloons, discarding opponents' loot, or messing with the board. **All abilities are mandatory** — you can't choose to skip one even if it hurts you.

One important detail: if a character gets discarded or removed before their daytime ability would trigger, that ability does **not** activate. Order matters.

#### Phase 3: Dusk — Collect Loot (High to Low)
This is where you grab treasure. Starting with the **highest-ranked** character and working down, each player picks **exactly 1 loot token** from that day's row on the island board. High-rank characters get first pick of the best loot — this is **their** advantage.

You **must** take a loot token if any are still available. You can't voluntarily pass.

#### Phase 4: Night — Night Abilities Resolve
Characters with a **night ability** (moon icon) now resolve, following the same low-to-high rank order as daytime. Night abilities often care about what loot you just collected or your current board position.

#### Phase 5: Rest
All played character cards move to each player's **ship** (the area in front of you). Characters sitting in your ship may have ongoing effects or **anchor abilities** that trigger at the end of the voyage. Then you move on to the next day.

subtopics.title: Voyages & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### End of a Voyage
Once all days in a voyage are finished, resolve these steps in order:

1. **Anchor abilities** — Characters in your ship and loot tokens with an anchor icon resolve their end-of-voyage effects. You choose the order for your own abilities. These can grant bonus doubloons, penalize opponents, or score based on your collected loot.
2. **Score dial update** — Add your current doubloon total to your **score dial**, then discard those physical doubloon tokens. **The score dial total is permanent** — once doubloons are locked into the dial, no game effect can take them away.
3. **Discard loot** — Return all your loot tokens to the bag (unless a specific character or loot ability says to keep something).
4. **Ship to graveyard** — Move all characters from your ship to your graveyard tile (face-up, visible to everyone). **Keep all remaining character cards in your hand** — they carry over to the next voyage.
5. **Reputation income** — At the start of the next voyage, each player earns doubloons based on their position on the reputation track. Higher positions pay more.
6. **Deal new characters** — Draw 6 new characters using the same process: randomly select 6 from one player's remaining deck, and everyone finds those same 6 in their own decks.
7. **Prepare loot** — Draw loot tokens for the new voyage (5 rows for Voyage 2, 6 rows for Voyage 3), with tokens equal to the player count in each row.

#### Loot Token Types (Calm Side)
Here's what each of the 7 loot types does on the Calm side:
- **Chest** — Worth **5 doubloons** at end of voyage. The best straightforward loot you can grab.
- **Map** — These reward set collection: **7 doubloons per pair** of maps, or **12 doubloons for a set of 3**. Accumulating maps across voyages is very powerful.
- **Amulet** — Worth **3 doubloons** at end of voyage. Solid, but less than a chest.
- **Barrel** — Flexible: gain **1 reputation** at end of voyage, OR gain **1 doubloon**. Nice when you want to climb the reputation track.
- **Hook** — Choose one: **keep a character in your ship** for the next voyage (skipping the graveyard), OR gain **2 doubloons**. Strong for preserving a powerful anchor ability across voyages.
- **Saber** — Lets you **discard another player's character** from the island. Aggressive and situational — great for removing a threat before their ability triggers.
- **Relic** — You **lose 3 doubloons** at end of voyage. This is the cursed loot nobody wants — but some characters specifically benefit from having relics, so it's not always a disaster.

The **Storm side** of the board changes these effects, making everything more aggressive and unpredictable.

**Growing Hands:** You keep **unplayed character cards** between voyages. In Voyage 1, you start with 6 cards and play 4 (keeping 2). Before Voyage 2, you receive 6 new cards added to your remaining hand. Before Voyage 3, you get 6 more. Your options expand each voyage — but so does the information your opponents have about what you might play.

#### After Voyage 3
The game ends after the 3rd voyage's anchor abilities and final score dial update. The player with the **most doubloons on their score dial wins**. (If your score exceeds 99, set the dial to 99 and use doubloon tokens to represent the extra.)

**Tiebreaker:** Higher reputation wins.

subtopics.title: Reputation & Graveyard
subtopics.content:
#### The Reputation Track
The reputation track is a row of **6 spaces** on the island board. All player tokens live on this track at all times — when yours moves right, others may slide left to make room (and vice versa). Reputation does three things for you:

1. **Tie-breaking during play** — When two players play the same ranked character on the same day, the player with higher reputation goes first. This matters for both daytime abilities and dusk loot selection.
2. **Voyage income** — Each space on the track shows a doubloon number. At the start of each new voyage, you collect doubloons matching your track position. Higher reputation = more income.
3. **Final tiebreaker** — If two players end the game with the same doubloon total, higher reputation wins.

Your reputation moves up or down based on character abilities and loot effects. Some characters reward high reputation; others benefit from being low on the track.

**Overflow rule:** If you'd gain reputation but you're already at the rightmost space, you gain **1 doubloon for each reputation** you can't receive. If you'd lose reputation at the leftmost space, you **lose 1 doubloon for each reputation** you can't lose. (This means the track edges convert reputation into doubloons in either direction.)

#### The Graveyard
Your graveyard tile is where characters go to rest after their voyage is done:
- Characters move from your ship to your **graveyard** at the end of each voyage, placed face-up.
- All graveyards are **public information** — anyone can look at any player's graveyard at any time.
- Some characters have **graveyard abilities** that trigger when they enter the graveyard or interact with characters already there.
- Tracking opponents' graveyards is your best deduction tool — you can figure out which characters they still hold by process of elimination.

#### Key Mechanism: Card Memory
Since all players receive the same characters each voyage, you can **track what opponents have played** by watching their graveyards. After Voyage 1, if an opponent played characters 5, 12, 18, and 23 (and kept 2), you know exactly which 4 are in their graveyard — and you can narrow down the 2 they still hold.

This deduction gets richer each voyage as graveyards fill up. By Voyage 3, you'll have strong reads on what everyone is holding.

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### The Core Tension: Rank vs. Timing
Every character card pulls you in two directions. High-rank characters pick loot **first** at dusk (best selection) but resolve daytime abilities **last** (most exposed to disruption). Low-rank characters act **first** during daytime (powerful offensive strikes) but get **last pick** of loot.

**The question you'll ask every single day:** Do you play high to grab the best loot, or play low to strike first with a strong ability?

#### Read the Loot Before You Pick
Before choosing your character, **always look at what loot tokens are sitting in today's row**.
- If the loot is excellent (chests worth 5 doubloons, maps for set collection), play a **high-rank character** to snag the best piece at dusk.
- If the loot is mediocre (barrels, amulets) or includes relics (-3 doubloons), play a **low-rank character** with a strong daytime ability instead. You're not missing much at dusk, and you get to disrupt your opponents before they can react.

#### The Mirroring Problem
Here's the catch: since everyone has the same hand, everyone is staring at the same loot. When the loot is great, **everyone** wants to play high. This creates a pile-up at the top of the rank order.

This means a medium-rank character might actually get surprisingly good loot — because all the high-rank characters are fighting each other for first pick.

**Counter-play tip:** When the loot is obviously excellent, consider playing a medium-rank card. Your opponents will burn their best characters fighting over first pick, and you'll grab a solid piece without wasting your strongest card.

#### Saving vs. Spending
You keep unplayed characters between voyages. Every card you play eventually goes to your graveyard — gone forever. Every card you save carries forward into a bigger hand next voyage.

- **Save your strongest characters** for high-value loot days or critical late-voyage moments.
- **Don't hoard everything** — unplayed characters score 0 doubloons. You have to play cards to earn points.
- In Voyage 1 (6 cards, 4 days), you'll play 4 and keep 2. Voyage 2 runs 5 days, and Voyage 3 runs 6, so manage your hand size carefully as voyages get longer.

#### Reputation Matters More Than You Think
Reputation breaks ties whenever two players play the same rank. If you and an opponent both play character 22, higher reputation picks loot first. Push your reputation up when you can — it provides consistent advantages in close matchups.

#### Relics Are Not Always Bad
Relics cost you 3 doubloons, but some characters specifically benefit from holding relics. If your hand includes characters that profit from curses, deliberately collecting relics can be a strong contrarian play that nobody sees coming.

#### Reputation Is Income
Don't neglect the reputation track — it pays doubloon income at the start of each new voyage. Over 2 income phases (before Voyages 2 and 3), a high reputation position compounds significantly. And once you max out the track, any overflow reputation converts directly to doubloons.

#### Watch the Graveyards
After each voyage, scan what characters your opponents have played. If you see that someone already burned their strongest daytime-ability character, you can play more aggressively on future days without fear of retaliation. Graveyards are public for a reason — use them.

#### Common Mistakes
- **Always playing your highest card.** Predictable and wasteful — you'll burn strong characters on mediocre loot days.
- **Ignoring relics.** Taking a relic when nothing in your hand benefits from it is -3 doubloons. But sometimes grabbing a relic to **deny** an opponent who benefits from curses is the correct play.
- **Ignoring reputation income.** Reputation isn't just a tiebreaker — it pays doubloons every voyage. Investing early pays dividends.
- **Not planning across voyages.** Characters you save in Voyage 1 combine with new cards in Voyage 2. Think ahead about which characters you want available later.
- **Forgetting anchor abilities.** Characters in your ship trigger anchor effects at voyage end — some of these are game-swinging. Plan around them, not just around loot.
- **Playing scared.** Sometimes the right move is playing a low-rank character specifically to grab a relic before an opponent who would benefit from it.

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### Your First Game of Libertalia

**Setup**: Everyone receives the same 6 character cards. Take a moment to look at your hand — you'll see 6 characters, each with a rank number, a name, and abilities marked with timing icons (sun for daytime, sunset for dusk, moon for night, anchor for end-of-voyage). Over on the island board, you'll see rows of face-up loot tokens — one row for each day, with tokens equal to the number of players in each row.

**Voyage 1, Day 1**: Start by looking at the loot tokens in Day 1's row. Figure out which pieces are the most valuable and which ones you'd rather avoid. Now look at your 6 characters and pick one that fits the situation:
- Excellent loot available? Play a high-rank character so you pick first at dusk.
- Mediocre loot or relics? Play a low-rank character with a strong daytime ability — you'll strike first and aren't missing much at dusk.

Place your chosen character face-down. Once everyone's ready, reveal simultaneously. Then resolve daytime abilities from low rank to high, collect loot from high rank to low, and finally resolve night abilities.

**Days 2-4**: Repeat the same process each day. Your hand shrinks as you play cards. By Day 4, you'll have 3 cards left — play 1 and keep 2 for the next voyage.

**End of Voyage 1**: Now resolve anchor abilities on all characters sitting in your ship. Then resolve loot effects — chests add 5 doubloons each, maps score in pairs (7 doubloons) or sets of 3 (12 doubloons), relics subtract 3 each. **Add your doubloon total to your score dial** (this number is now locked in permanently), then return your physical doubloon tokens and loot to the supply. Move your ship characters to your graveyard.

**Voyage 2**: First, collect reputation income based on your track position. Then deal 6 new characters (same process — one player draws randomly, everyone matches). You also keep your 2 unplayed cards from Voyage 1, so you start Voyage 2 with 8 cards. This voyage lasts **5 days** — one longer than Voyage 1. New loot tokens get drawn for each day's row.

**Voyage 3**: Collect reputation income again. Deal 6 more new characters added to your remaining hand (which could be 9+ cards by now). Voyage 3 lasts **6 days** — the longest voyage. You'll have maximum flexibility but also maximum deduction opportunity. Check everyone's graveyards to narrow down what they're holding.

**Final Scoring**: After Voyage 3's anchor abilities and final score dial update, the player with the most doubloons on their score dial wins. For your first game, 30-50 doubloons is a solid target depending on player count.

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Reputation Track Manipulation
The reputation track has only **6 spaces**, which makes every position shift meaningful. Reputation controls tiebreakers when players pick the same character and determines your doubloon income at the start of each voyage. **Key insight**: reputation swings are most valuable early in a voyage because more days remain where that tie-breaking advantage applies. Push reputation before big loot days, not after them.

Don't forget the **overflow rule** — once you're at the top of the track, any additional reputation you'd gain converts to doubloons instead. This means characters that boost reputation are never truly wasted, even when you're already leading.

#### Hand Reading
All players receive the same 6 characters each voyage, and you keep unplayed cards from previous voyages. **Track what your opponents play by watching their graveyards.** If someone played their rank 35 character on Day 1, they can't play it again. By the later days of a voyage, you can predict opponents' plays with increasing accuracy — especially in Voyage 3, when graveyards are full of information.

The strongest reads come from knowing which characters someone **didn't** play. If they held back two cards from Voyage 1, you know exactly which 6 were dealt. Cross-reference with their graveyard and you've narrowed their options significantly.

#### Loot Valuation
Not all loot is created equal, and values shift based on what you already have. Here's how to think about each type:
- **Chests** are a flat **5 doubloons** — always solid, never bad.
- **Maps** reward accumulation: **7 doubloons per pair, 12 per set of 3**. A single map is worth nothing at voyage end, but pairs and triples are among the highest-value loot in the game. Prioritize maps when you already have one.
- **Amulets** are a safe **3 doubloons** — take them when nothing better is available.
- **Relics** cost you **3 doubloons**, but taking one to deny an opponent whose characters benefit from curses can be a net-positive play.
- **Sabers** let you discard an opponent's character — devastating when timed to remove a character before its anchor ability triggers.
- **Hooks** let you preserve a character across voyages, which is strongest when paired with powerful anchor abilities.

Always value loot based on your current collection and board state, not in isolation.

#### Day Order Manipulation
The rank system creates a deliberate tension. High-rank characters pick the **best loot** at dusk (they choose first) but resolve daytime abilities **last** (lowest to highest). Low-rank characters resolve daytime abilities **first** (great for aggressive plays) but pick loot **last** at dusk.

This duality means you sometimes **want** to play low even when the loot is decent — because striking first with a daytime ability can remove an opponent's character before it ever collects loot. Think about which phase matters more on a given day.

#### Voyage Pacing
Voyages grow longer as the game progresses: **4 days, then 5, then 6**. Use the early days of each voyage to build position — collect barrels for reputation, grab hooks to preserve anchor characters, and play expendable low-rank characters. Save your strongest characters for the **final days of each voyage**, when the stakes are highest and your opponents have already committed their best cards.

In Voyage 3 (6 days), you have more room to set up early and close strong. The last 2 days of the final voyage are often where games are won or lost.
